pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: The patient is experiencing an acute neuro-visceral crisis consistent with Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP). THE SMOKING GUN: While the clinical triad of acute abdominal pain, psychiatric symptoms, and neuropathy strongly suggested this diagnosis, the definitive laboratory clue was the observation that a sample of her urine, collected late on Day 3, had turned a distinctive dark, port-wine color after standing exposed to sunlight for several hours. This color change confirmed the presence of large quantities of porphyrin precursors. Specific quantitative testing revealed a massive elevation of urinary Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA). Further genetic testing revealed a heterozygous mutation in the HMBS gene. We also identified that, seven days prior to admission, Ms. Vance had begun a low-carbohydrate, calorie-restricted diet and had been prescribed Phenobarbital for her anxiety (a known potent inducer of hepatic CYP450 enzymes and Porphyria crises). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Acute Intermittent Porphyria is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a deficiency in the enzyme Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS), also known as Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD), the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. Normally, this deficiency is asymptomatic (latent phase). However, when the heme demands increase (due to drugs, fasting, or hormones), the bottleneck in the pathway leads to the massive accumulation of the upstream neurotoxic precursors, ALA and PBG. ALA is structurally similar to GABA, and its accumulation is thought to contribute directly to the severe central and peripheral neurological dysfunction, autonomic instability (leading to the tachycardia, hypertension, and inappropriate ADH secretion causing refractory hyponatremia), and the characteristic abdominal pain, which is neuropathic rather than inflammatory or surgical. Treatment involves immediate cessation of the offending drug and administration of intravenous Heme Argonate (Panhematin) to suppress ALA synthase activity and thus reduce the production of toxic precursors.
